content.text: statements inside JSX — JSX is an expression, and
is a statement. Three operators fill this gap: the ternary operator (
). Together they cover the vast majority of conditional rendering patterns in React.
The ternary operator is a compact
that evaluates to a value. It takes three parts: a condition, the value if true, and the value if false.
In JSX, wrap the ternary in
When the "else" case renders nothing, return
Ternaries can be nested for multiple conditions, but beyond two levels they become hard to read. At that point, extract the logic into a variable or use early returns:

operator evaluates its left side first. If the left side is falsy, it returns the left side immediately without evaluating the right side (short-circuiting). If the left side is truthy, it returns the right side.

// Returns the first falsy value, or the last value if all are truthy
true && 'hello'     // 'hello'
'foo' && 'bar'      // 'bar'
false && 'hello'    // false  (short-circuits)
0 && 'hello'        // 0      (short-circuits)
null && 'hello'     // null   (short-circuits)
undefined && run()  // undefined, run() never called
is used for "render this only if the condition is true":
mistake in React: when the left side is
, and React renders it as the visible character "0":
value, or the last value if all are falsy. It is used for default/fallback values:
operator (nullish coalescing) only falls back when the left side is
. This makes it safer for numeric or boolean props:

// || treats 0, '', false as falsy — falls back for them
0 || 'default'      // 'default' — probably not what you want for a score!

// ?? only falls back for null/undefined
0 ?? 'default'      // 0          — keeps the valid 0
'' ?? 'default'     // ''         — keeps the empty string
null ?? 'default'   // 'default'  — correct fallback
undefined ?? 'default' // 'default' — correct fallback
) is a closely related operator that short-circuits when accessing properties on
Render A or B based on condition
Render A or nothing (when A is not 0)
Render A or nothing (when condition might be 0)
Default value (any falsy value triggers fallback)
Safe property access on possibly-null object
is the standard for if/else in JSX. Use it to render one component or another.
is for "render this or nothing." Beware: when the left side is
, React renders the character "0." Always guard with an explicit boolean check when working with numbers.
is for fallback values when any falsy value should trigger the fallback. Returns the first truthy value.
(nullish coalescing) is for fallback values only when
(optional chaining) safely accesses nested properties on
objects without throwing a TypeError — returns
All short-circuit operators return values, not booleans — which is why they work inside JSX expressions.
